items.title: Meet Minimum Set-Aside Requirement by End of First Credit Year
items.action: Confirm that the required percentage of rent-restricted units (20/50, 40/60, 25/60 for NYC, or average income test) is met before the close of the first year of the credit period for every building.
items.consequence: Failing to meet the minimum set-aside by year-end disqualifies the project from claiming any credit in year one AND all future years — a total loss of the LIHTC benefit.

items.title: Verify Deep Rent Skewed 15-40 Election Before Making It — It Is Irrevocable
items.action: Before electing the deep rent skewed 15-40 test, conduct a long-term feasibility review and set up unit-level monitoring to ensure at least 15% of low-income units remain occupied by tenants at or below 40% AMI for the entire compliance period.
items.consequence: Once made, this election cannot be revoked; permanent non-compliance exposes the project to credit recapture and IRS penalty without any corrective exit option.

items.title: Ensure All Information on Form 8609 Is Accurate Before Submission
items.action: Implement an internal review process to verify all income limits, set-aside elections, unit designations, and dates on Form 8609 are correct and fully documented before filing.
items.consequence: Providing false information on Form 8609 exposes the filer to IRS penalties and potential referral to the Department of Justice for civil or criminal litigation.

items.title: Submit Original Signed Form 8609 to IRS LIHC Unit by First 8609-A Filing Deadline
items.action: Mail the original signed Form 8609 to the IRS LIHC Unit in Philadelphia no later than the due date (including extensions) of the first tax return with which Form 8609-A is filed.
items.consequence: Missing this one-time submission deadline can delay or invalidate the credit claim for the building, jeopardizing the entire credit stream.

items.title: File Form 8609-A Annually for Each Year of the 15-Year Compliance Period
items.action: Set up a recurring annual filing process to submit Form 8609-A for every building for each of the 15 compliance years, attached to the owner's tax return.
items.consequence: Failure to file Form 8609-A annually breaks the compliance certification chain and can result in credit disallowance or recapture.

items.title: NYC Projects Must Use the 25-60 Test, Not the 40-60 Test
items.action: For any building located in New York City, reconfigure compliance tracking systems to apply the 25-60 test (at least 25% of units occupied by residents at or below 60% AMI) instead of the standard 40-60 test.
items.consequence: Using the wrong set-aside test for an NYC project constitutes non-compliance and can result in credit disqualification for the affected building.

items.title: Confirm Average Income Test Election Was Made After March 23, 2018
items.action: Review the election date for any project using the average income test and confirm it was formally elected after March 23, 2018.
items.consequence: An average income test election made on or before March 23, 2018 is invalid, placing the project in set-aside non-compliance and jeopardizing all credits claimed under that election.

items.title: Verify Average Income Test Unit Designations Are in Valid 10% Increments and Average Does Not Exceed 60% AMI
items.action: For average income test projects, confirm each unit's designated imputed income limitation is set at 20%, 30%, 40%, 50%, 60%, 70%, or 80% AMI, and that the project-wide average does not exceed 60% AMI.
items.consequence: Incorrect unit designation increments or an average exceeding 60% AMI constitutes non-compliance and can result in loss of low-income unit status for affected units.

items.title: Include Valid Taxpayer Identification Number on All Form 8609 Submissions
items.action: Verify that a valid SSN, EIN, or ITIN is included on every Form 8609 submission before filing.
items.consequence: Missing or incorrect taxpayer identification numbers can delay or prevent IRS processing of the credit claim.

items.title: Apply Correct Over-Income (140%/170%) Rule for Continuing Residents
items.action: For each building, confirm whether the standard 140% rule or the deep rent skewed 170% rule applies when a continuing resident's income rises above the applicable limit, and update unit tracking accordingly.
items.consequence: Misapplying the over-income threshold could cause a unit to be incorrectly counted as a low-income unit, creating a set-aside shortfall and potential credit recapture.

items.title: Apply National Non-Metropolitan Median Income for Qualifying Rural Projects
items.action: For rural buildings with a credit allocation and income determinations made after July 30, 2008, compare national non-metropolitan median income to area median gross income and use whichever is higher.
items.consequence: Using the lower area median instead of the national non-metropolitan median for eligible rural projects may incorrectly exclude tenants who qualify, reducing the project's qualifying unit count.

items.title: Check Carryover Allocation 10% Basis Test and Placed-in-Service Deadlines for Disaster-Period Extensions
items.action: For any building with a carryover allocation whose 10% basis test or placed-in-service deadline fell between April 1, 2020 and December 31, 2023, verify whether a COVID/disaster extension applies and confirm the adjusted deadline.
items.consequence: Missing an applicable extended placed-in-service or 10% test deadline — even by one day — can void the carryover allocation and eliminate the credit for that building.

items.title: Obtain Corrected Form 8609 from Agency If Placed-in-Service Date Is Wrong or Missing
items.action: Before completing Part II of Form 8609, verify the placed-in-service date is correct; if it is wrong or blank, request a new or amended Form 8609 from the housing credit agency before proceeding.
items.consequence: Filing Part II with an incorrect or missing placed-in-service date creates a reporting error that may require amended filings and could delay credit processing.

items.title: Issue a Separate Form 8609 for Each Building and Each Separate Allocation
items.action: Confirm that each building in a multi-building project, each separate calendar-year allocation, and each separate acquisition/rehabilitation allocation has its own Form 8609.
items.consequence: Combining multiple buildings or allocations on a single Form 8609 is a reporting error that can cause misalignment between allocation records and IRS filings.

items.title: Confirm Applicable Credit Percentage (4% or 9%) Is Correctly Identified on Form 8609
items.action: For each building, verify whether the 4% minimum floor (post-2020 federally subsidized or bond-financed buildings) or the 9% minimum (new non-federally subsidized buildings placed in service after July 30, 2008) applies, and confirm the correct rate is reflected on Form 8609.
items.consequence: Using the wrong applicable credit percentage understates or overstates the credit, which can result in recapture, penalties, or IRS examination.

items.title: Verify That All Claimed Low-Income Units Are Suitable for Occupancy and Used on a Non-Transient Basis
items.action: Audit all units claimed as low-income units to confirm they meet the habitability standard and are not used for transient occupancy, unless a recognized exception (e.g., transitional housing for the homeless) applies.
items.consequence: Units that do not meet the suitability or non-transient use requirement cannot count toward the minimum set-aside, potentially causing a set-aside shortfall.

items.title: Retain All Required Records for 3 Years After Compliance Period Tax Return Due Date
items.action: Establish a records retention policy covering Form 8609, all Forms 8609-A, Forms 8586, Forms 8611, binding agreements, election statements, and allocation documents, retained for 3 years after the due date of the return for the tax year that includes the end of the 15-year compliance period.
items.consequence: Inadequate recordkeeping creates audit vulnerability and can result in the IRS disallowing credits or imposing recapture if documentation cannot be produced.

items.title: Confirm Housing Credit Agency Has Filed Form 8609 Copy with Form 8610
items.action: Coordinate with the housing credit agency to confirm it has completed and signed Part I of Form 8609, submitted a copy with Form 8610, retained a copy, and forwarded the original signed Form 8609 to the building owner.
items.consequence: If the agency fails to properly complete its filing obligations, the allocation record may be incomplete, creating documentation gaps that complicate credit claims or audits.

items.title: Note That IRS May Share Filing Information with Department of Justice
items.action: Ensure all compliance documentation is accurate and complete, recognizing that information disclosed on Form 8609 and related filings may be referred to DOJ for civil or criminal proceedings.
items.consequence: Non-compliance discovered through IRS filings can escalate beyond tax penalties to federal civil or criminal litigation.

items.title: Student Rule Exceptions — Gap in Source Data, Human Review Required
items.action: Research applicable student rule exceptions under IRC Section 42 from primary sources, as no student rule guidance was present in the analyzed materials.
items.consequence: Without confirming student rule compliance, projects with student tenants may have incorrectly counted units toward the minimum set-aside, risking credit disqualification.

human_review_flags: Student rule exceptions were explicitly flagged as a gap in the income agent findings (confidence 0.55) — no student rule analysis was performed and this area requires independent research before relying on this checklist for projects with student tenants.
Household size-specific income limit tables were not provided in source materials — projects should verify current HUD income limits directly for their AMI calculations.

Rehabilitation expenditure deadline extensions (multiple date windows: April 1, 2020 through December 31, 2023) involve complex overlapping scenarios; manual verification of which window applies to each specific building is strongly recommended.
synthesis_notes:
• Risk score calculation: 3 HIGH items (anchor score 60) + 9 MEDIUM items (+12 points at ~1.3 each) + 3 LOW items (+2) + 1 gap item with low confidence (-2 offset) = 72. Score is below 80 because no active confirmed violation was reported — these are prospective compliance risks, not confirmed breaches.
• HIGH severity items are anchored exclusively to ViolationFindings flags LIHTC-001 (total credit disqualification), LIHTC-002 (irrevocable election), and LIHTC-003 (false information penalties), consistent with guard rail requiring a ViolationFindings anchor for HIGH.
• Form 8609 one-time submission and Form 8609-A annual filing were elevated to HIGH because deadline and reporting agents both confirmed them as foundational compliance obligations, and LIHTC-003 (false/incomplete reporting) provides a violation anchor.
• No conflicting findings were identified across agents — all four agents are consistent and complementary on shared topics (set-aside tests, over-income rules, recordkeeping, filing deadlines).
• COVID-19 extension deadlines (April 1, 2020 – December 31, 2022/2023) are noted in both the deadline and income agents and are confirmed expired; they are flagged under the carryover allocation item as historical lookback items requiring verification for any still-open prior allocations.
• Student rule gap is the only item below 0.7 confidence (0.55); flagged for human review per guard rail.
• Household size income limit tables are absent from all source materials — flagged as a data gap but not assigned a checklist item because no actionable finding can be anchored to it.
• Rehabilitation expenditure window items were consolidated into a single MEDIUM checklist item to avoid redundancy across the four overlapping date windows identified by the deadline agent.
